<findings>

[observation] [info] High cohesion (0.99) — files are tightly interconnected
[suggestion] [info] Consider extracting port allocation logic from port.ts into a reusable utility module that other packages could leverage for dynamic port management

</findings>

<insights>

- High cohesion (0.99) — files are tightly interconnected
- Acts as the primary gateway between external clients and internal domain packages
- Implements the gateway pattern with mcp-deps.ts concentrating all cross-package imports
- Provides clean separation between server infrastructure and presentation components
- Excellent cohesion (0.99) indicates a well-focused server infrastructure layer
- Gateway pattern successfully isolates cross-package dependencies in mcp-deps.ts
- Low coupling (0.01) demonstrates clean architectural boundaries with minimal external dependencies
- Serves as the architectural backbone connecting external clients to internal domain logic through well-defined HTTP and MCP endpoints
- Implements the adapter pattern through mcp-deps.ts to translate between web transport protocols and domain package APIs
- Gateway zone successfully implements the adapter pattern, translating between HTTP/MCP protocols and internal domain APIs
- Server infrastructure files follow consistent 'routes-*' naming pattern for HTTP handlers, establishing clear convention for endpoint organization
- Port allocation logic in port.ts demonstrates defensive programming with fallback mechanisms for dynamic port assignment
- Gateway module mcp-deps.ts uses descriptive naming that clearly indicates its role as MCP dependency concentrator
- Consider extracting port allocation logic from port.ts into a reusable utility module that other packages could leverage for dynamic port management
- [call graph] 837 internal calls, 21 outgoing, 1 incoming (cohesion: 0.98, coupling: 0.02)

</insights>
